nodes.name: 布宜诺斯艾利斯省
nodes.summary: 布宜诺斯艾利斯省是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联30种鸟类，包括棕头草雁、Red-and-white crake、查科林鴞等30种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 库斯科大区

nodes.name: LC
nodes.summary: LC表示 IUCN 无危等级，当前关联8208种鸟类，如鸵鸟、鸸鹋、南方鶴鴕等8208种鸟类。

nodes.name: 圣马丁大区

nodes.name: Bran-colored flycatcher
nodes.summary: Bran-colored flycatcher（Bran-colored flycatcher，学名 Myiophobus fasciatus），在图谱中标记为无危（LC）物种，已关联22个分布地点节点，主要威胁包括森林砍伐。

nodes.name: 森林砍伐
nodes.summary: 原生林被砍伐导致栖息地面积和连通性下降。

nodes.name: 玻利瓦州

nodes.name: 巴拿马

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: It is found in Costa Rica and Panama, on Trinidad, and in every mainland South American country except Chile

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: The subspecies are found thus: *Myiophobus fasciatus furfurosus: from southern San José Province in southwestern Costa Rica south through Puntarenas Province into Panama just past the Canal Zone and on the Pearl Islands off Panama's Pacific coast *Myiophobus fasciatus fasciatus: most of the length of the three ranges of the Colombian Andes; eastern Ecuador; Venezuela in the Serranía del Perijá, across most of the north, and western Amazonas and central Bolívar states (though probably throughout); coastally across the Guianas; northern Brazil in the states of Roraima, Amapá, and northernmost Pará *Myiophobus fasciatus saturatus: Amazonian and east Andean foothills Peru between San Martín and Cusco departments *Myiophobus fasciatus auriceps: southeastern Peru, Acre state in western Brazil, northern and eastern Bolivia, western Paraguay, and Argentina south to Buenos Aires Province *Myiophobus fasciatus flammiceps: approximately the eastern half of Brazil; eastern Paraguay; Uruguay; northeastern Argentina The bran-colored flycatcher inhabits a variety of landscapes across its wide range

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: It also inhabits successional vegetation on riverbanks and islands and the Llanos of Colombia and Venezuela

links.label: 保护等级
links.evidence: status = LC

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered fairly common in Costa Rica, common in most of Colombia but rarer in the east, "common but somewhat local" in Venezuela, rare in Ecuador, and fairly common in Peru. It occurs in many protected areas and "appears to thrive in shrubby successional vegetation that follows deforestation"
